reviews.author: Authentic Experience
reviews.text:
This museum is for the people who want to know the history of this beautiful city Zagreb. They are growing so I think in the future it will be more better.
You should visit once if you love the history and want to know vibrant history of the city.
Ticket price is really good and they were really kind. They have description in English also but unfortunately all of them don't have in English.
I hope they will improve it.
Altogether must visit place in the city if you want to know it's history.

reviews.author: Renato Tan
reviews.text:
If you want to get a glimpse of Zagreb’s history, this is the place to go. With 3 floors to visit and check out, this area will eat up to over an hour of your time.

Don’t miss out the actual writings on the wall.

reviews.author: Ramona H.
reviews.text: It is a nice museum which tells a lot about the history of Zagreb. There is so much to see. Unfortunately there is not everywhere a translation in english or any other language which makes it hard to understand everything. There are big panels which are explaining the theme of the room, this one is translate. But the individuel objects have no description in any other language then croation.

reviews.author: Phil Mead
reviews.text: The evolution of a city like Zagreb over more than few thousand years from Iron Age to the 20th century is not just about urban development but about the history of Croatia as a country too. Because the City Museum traces this development from Iron Age settlement to the 1990s there is something here to interest most adults with many entertaining exhibits, stories and photos. My personal favorites are the rooms about life in the 19th century with the rise of nationalism and expansion of Zagreb in to a modern metropolis. A great way to spend a few hours learning about your host nation and city on a rainy day!

reviews.author: John Bates
reviews.text: Tells the entire history of the region surrounding modern day Zagreb, from the iron age (includes an in-person excavation of the Late Bronze age/early iron age site in the NE of Zagreb) to the modern day, including Celtic, Medieval and later stages. Good collection of artifacts across the period. Will take about 1-2 hours to complete, and the price is extremely reasonable. One problem (and the reason for 3 stars) is that ~33% of the information boards were only in Croatian. Had to use ai to photo and translate, which isn't ideal.

reviews.author: Κατερινα Μπατσιουδη
reviews.text:
Review of the Archaeological Museum in Zagreb

The Archaeological Museum in Zagreb is a true hidden gem and an absolute delight for history lovers. It boasts an impressive and well-curated collection of artifacts ranging from Ancient Egypt to Classical Greece, the Roman era, and beyond.

The Greek section, in particular, stands out as one of the highlights of the museum. The collection includes beautifully crafted pottery, intricate jewelry, sculptural fragments, and votive offerings that reflect the sophistication and artistry of ancient Greek civilization. The explanatory texts are informative without being overwhelming, and the layout allows visitors to follow the development of Greek art and culture through the centuries. It was genuinely a surprise to see such a rich and well-preserved display of Hellenic history in Zagreb.

The Egyptian artifacts are also fascinating, with mummies, sarcophagi, and daily life items that offer a glimpse into the spiritual and material world of ancient Egypt. The Roman section is equally strong, including statues, inscriptions, and everyday objects that speak to the daily lives and beliefs of Roman citizens.

The museum building itself is elegant and atmospheric, enhancing the whole experience. The exhibits are thoughtfully arranged, and the transitions between historical periods feel natural and coherent. The staff are friendly, knowledgeable, and always willing to assist.

Overall, I highly recommend the Archaeological Museum of Zagreb to anyone visiting the city. Whether you’re deeply interested in ancient history or just want a meaningful cultural experience, this museum is definitely worth your time.

reviews.author: D V
reviews.text: Great museum! I especially like Egyptian, Roman and Slavic area.

reviews.author: Filipe ZG
reviews.text: The Archaeological Museum in Zagreb is a very well-curated and interesting museum that offers a deep dive into Croatia's rich history. It's an absolute must-see for anyone interested in the ancient past, from prehistoric times through the Roman era and beyond. The collection of artifacts is impressive and diverse, and the exhibits are thoughtfully organized, making it easy to follow the historical narrative. While the museum is excellent, it could benefit from more interactive elements or a bit more modern presentation in some areas to fully engage a wider audience. However, the quality of the artifacts and the overall historical value more than make up for it.

reviews.author: Hamy Nguyen
reviews.text: this is a great archeological museum rich in history and cultures! the staff was friendly, helpful and most importantly kept things in place. there were a lot of informational texts about the certain periods and artefacts, however they were awfully long and that makes it a bit hard to gather good intel if you are interested in a lot of topics.

reviews.author: Aaron Rose
reviews.text: Far less impressive than other European archeological museums go. The extremely detailed info plaques are a huge plus (all with English translations!) but you won't find anything you can't find in other, larger museums. For a museum this small, space is also not used most effectively, such as an entire floor dedicated to relatively modern Asian dresses.

places.name: Zagreb 80's museum
places.address: 1st floor, Radićeva 34, 10000, Gornji Grad - Medveščak, Zagreb, Croatia

reviews.author: Jürgen Engel
reviews.text: If you like the 80s, especially if you grew up in that area, you MUST see it. Soooo many beautiful memories. You can touch and try everything even old computers and do many things. I am very touched.

reviews.author: Robert Quinn
reviews.text: Very fun, we had a blast.  We spent maybe 30-40 minutes there, well worth 5 euros (ea).  A working 128k  computer, video games and some fun displays. Cool posters, books, etc.

reviews.author: Vanesa Nikolova
reviews.text: A hidden gem in the heart of Zagreb. Brings out warm childhood memories for everyone that has grown up in a (post)socialist state. You can touch everything and there is the cute dog companion Luna wandering around from time to time. Recommended!

reviews.author: Paulina W.
reviews.text: Small museum with old gadgets. A few rooms like kitchen, living room, bedroom. You can play the piano, arcade, touch things. A little bit overpriced (8€ for adults)

reviews.author: Török Máté
reviews.text: It was a great disappoitment. As on Google the opening hours are till 10 pm, on site you can see papers with closing time of 9 pm. Though we arrived at 8 pm and a handwritten paper was hanging only in Croatian informing about having finished the work at 7 pm. The homepage shown on Google is not actual (with prices in kuna), the phone number given on Google is out of use. We had no chance to reach the operator.

reviews.author: Milad Maleki
reviews.text:
We visited the Museum of Broken Relationships, and it was completely different from what we expected, in a very interesting way. The museum is based on people donating one item from a relationship that has ended, along with a short note explaining its story.

Some stories are heartbreaking, some funny, and some quite hard to understand, but that’s part of what makes the experience so unique and human. In one word: different and definitely worth visiting.

One suggestion for improvement would be adding an audio guide, perhaps with the voices of the people who donated the items. That could make the stories even more powerful and personal.

reviews.author: Ivy Summer
reviews.text: WOW—highly recommend! The museum changes the exhibit once every 4-5 years. I went because it’s my annual solo trip and I’d never been to Croatia before and it was cold and rainy and the concept is so amazing. It exceeds expectations by not only tugging on the heart strings but also making you cackle, laughing out loud unexpectedly. It’s the experience of being part of someone’s life when they lost (any kind of) love at some point, and it’s a wonderful emotional rollercoaster of an experience. At the end of my 2-hour visit, I donated a letter that I’ll never send to my ex husband, which the museum graciously accepted. It was my official way of closing a chapter of my life in a most serendipitous way because I’d intended to burn the letter but was never ready until I discovered this unique museum. It’s a must-see. Thank you to the creators of this museum for an opportunity to let go and move on. Sidenote: the adjacent cafe is a nice place to decompress with a coffee or drink and the gift shop is really cool!! Definitely bought a few things there.

reviews.author: Linda P
reviews.text:
A must see!

A unique museum collection containing various items “of one’s affection” that at some point required dismissal for resolution.
Donor’s also offer a vignette of the relationship behind the objects.

Decide for yourself, if and where you fit into these stories  , and have a cathartic experience for a few hours.

There are 5-6 small rooms , lots of interesting readings that require 1-2 hours of your time.
Clever idea to put together this collection. One that will keep growing as long as humanity is involved!

reviews.author: Maureen Parker
reviews.text: Wonderful little museum filled with mementos and poignant stories about the people, things, events and places that we fall in love with and have to let go. If you enjoy stories about being human and want to experience a different kind of museum filled with humanity then you must go. I thoroughly enjoyed! Spent about 1-1.5 hours there. Nice, comfortable cafe attached. This is in historic old town or upper town

reviews.author: Catalina Bogzoiu
reviews.text: If you’re looking to see something unique and interesting, I highly recommend visiting Museum of Broken Relationships. You can read all kinds of stories behind broken relationships—some are absolutely hilarious, while others are surprisingly touching and offer meaningful insights into human connections. It’s a memorable experience and definitely worth a visit.

reviews.author: Aaron Nuckolls
reviews.text: Definitely a must do if you are in the area. A fun, interactive experience with a little bit of history added in! Dunja was an amazing worker and made the tour even better.

reviews.author: Delia
reviews.text: The museum is a very good choice for a fun activity in Zagreb. Very interactive with lots of different rooms that represent the stages you go through a night of drinking, at the end we received a shot of rakia. Definitely recommend it!

reviews.author: Asia Chhon
reviews.text: Cute, gun experience! Small museum with lots of fun stories and the staff was really nice. Definitely a good way to stay out of the sun and have a fun experience. Bought my ticket online for a small discount!

reviews.author: Signe Homčenko
reviews.text: I loved it. I recomend to go and see it😅 for better experience you can get a drink at the bigining and then go through the rooms, read some funny drinking stories and take some funny pictures, try to step on a platform and go on straight line with drunk goggles and some more😅

reviews.author: Nas Sol
reviews.text: An amusing museum of some very funny stories that are well written. And you can walk away feeling a buzz. This is an interesting museum.

reviews.author: Georgi Zlatanov
reviews.text: Really fun place to visit in Zagreb! The Museum of Illusions has many interactive exhibits and optical illusions that make you question what you see. It’s great both for taking photos and for learning how different illusions work. The staff was friendly and the museum is well organized. Also, they offer a student discount, which is a nice bonus. Definitely worth visiting if you’re in the city.

reviews.author: Ramona H.
reviews.text:
A small but fascinating museum. It is very exciting to see how the brain can be deceived by illusions. It is very interactive and therefore just as suitable for adults as it is for children. The shop sells little logic games, etc.

The museum is somewhat hidden away; the entrance is located in the passageway to the backyard. Unfortunately, the cloakroom is rather small, and I had to get toilet paper from the men's restroom.

reviews.author: Zoja Fras
reviews.text: It was a nice experience but we were expecting a little bit more. Its a small place and since it was a bizzy day there was alot of wating to see the illusions. After all i think its a nice experinece but just remember that its quite small.

reviews.author: Parisa De
reviews.text: Very nice and friendly staff today. The museum itself which was 12euro per person was too limited in comparison with other museum of illusions or teamlabs, therefore I think a ticket of 5 euro would have been a better and suitable price for what you get. Many stuff are also worn out or dirty looking . I’m sure in time they can get better and expand maybe to a better location with more room and maybe a renovation. Thanks

reviews.author: G Z
reviews.text: Hidden in an alleyway with a dark entrance, it was difficult for my mum (a senior) to climb up the stairs due to the low visibility. This museum is quite quaint and small, and staff are friendly. However I probably wouldn't visit again given we saw the entire museum and interacted with the exhibits in under an hour.

reviews.author: Jesse Sharpe
reviews.text:
Such a cool museum! Every exhibit is by the same artist who has done extraordinary work collecting literally hundreds of stories and myths from Croatian oral tradition. Every room is meticulously themed. While the art is amazing, I was most impressed by the way he had painstakingly made dioramas and models which combine to theme each room into a perfect environment to consume the stories. Absolutely stunning.

The staff was also wonderful, especially the woman who welcomed us at the counter and the dog who belonged to the artist!

reviews.author: Kenna Wood
reviews.text: Gem of a museum. Interesting history of Croatian folktales with amazing art throughout the exhibits. Audio guide in multiple languages that is easy to use and very informative. Highly recommend

reviews.author: Nataliya Obrevko
reviews.text:
It’s incredibly cool, immersive, and interactive! Under every painting or installation there’s a button. You lift your audio guide, press it, and suddenly you’re pulled straight into the story. Witches, fairies, dragons, thieves, forgotten legends. You don’t just look at the exhibits, you fall into them. I spent almost two hours inside and didn’t want to leave! I LOVED IT!
If you’re in Zagreb and want something magical, atmospheric, and quietly addictive, this place delivers. It feels like a dream you’re not ready to wake up from.

reviews.author: Lily Gunawan
reviews.text:
Needed a lot of reading to enjoy. For me this is a bit creepy as i dont enjoy dark mood. But the art are good and arranged in nice details.
Some people who enjoy the readings may need about 2 hours.
For me that does not enjoy dark mood, i need less than 30 minutes.

reviews.author: Beyza Aytaç
reviews.text:
We recently visited the Museum of Lost Tales, and although the concept is creative and the atmosphere is charming, the overall experience didn’t fully match what we personally look for in a museum visit. The exhibition is built around Croatian folktales, with miniatures, atmospheric scenography, and visual storytelling elements. This style may appeal especially to families with kids, fans of fantasy-inspired photography, and visitors who enjoy folklore or imaginative displays — though it’s important to say that the museum is not designed as a children’s attraction. It simply has a presentation style that resonates more with those groups.

In our case, the visit felt shorter, less interactive, and less immersive than we expected. Online photos create the impression of a larger, more dynamic space, but most of the displays are static and focused on ambiance rather than engagement. We completed the entire museum much earlier than the 1.5–2 hours mentioned in other reviews, and we felt that the storytelling elements didn’t unfold in a way that kept us fully engaged.

The ticket price was €12 per person (€24 for two), and considering the length and depth of our visit, we didn’t feel that the value matched the experience. This is of course a subjective perspective — the museum simply didn’t align with our museum preferences, which usually include more interactivity, more narrative depth, or larger-scale installations.

For potential visitors, it may help to say:
• This museum might be suitable for people who enjoy folklore, miniatures, atmospheric sets, fantasy-style photography, or exploring visual interpretations of myths.
• It may be less suitable for visitors looking for hands-on interaction, extensive exhibitions, educational depth, or longer museum experiences.

We’re sharing this not as a criticism of the concept itself, but simply to help others with similar expectations understand what the experience is like

reviews.author: Ivan Milić
reviews.text:
This museum will be very interesting to anyone who has ever played any videogame in their life. It covers all the games and related paraphernalia from the 1970 onwards. You will find many old arcades, game consoles from the 90's and modern games. All the items have a brief description in English and Croatian. You can also play many of the games.
Guaranteed fun for at least two hours! Also, the gift shop is very interesting. It's possible to pay with the credit card.

reviews.author: Hristo Hristov (Christo Christoff)
reviews.text: Neither “supreme“ nor "magnificent" are words that can do this museum justice. The founders have obviously gone at incredible lengths acquiring and preserving in immaculate condition exceptional pieces of digital history. The museum covers the history of gaming and the important milestones from the 70s until present days. The curation of the artifacts and the overall ambience is also impressive. The best part? The museum offers the opportunity to play some of the games!

reviews.author: Олег Тут
reviews.text:
A True Pilgrimage for Every Gamer – A Journey Through Time! ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
​As a gamer with 35 years of experience, I thought I had seen it all, but the Video Game History Museum in Zagreb completely blew me away. I have been here for over an hour and I’ve only just made it to the second room—that is how immersive and fascinating the collection is!
​This isn't just a display of old plastic; it’s like touching the very DNA of the gaming industry. Seeing the Computer Space (1971) cabinet—the world’s first commercial arcade game—was a spiritual experience. The museum perfectly preserves the legacy of pioneers like Ralph Baer and Nolan Bushnell, showcasing everything from the iconic Atari Pong (1972) to the world's first handheld cartridge system, the Microvision (1979).
​Why you should visit:
​Depth of History: They cover everything from early experiments like Spacewar! to the home console revolution.
​Rare Artifacts: You can see rare pieces of tech that you usually only find in history books.
​Atmosphere: It’s a place where you can truly "touch history" and appreciate the evolution of the medium we love.
​Whether you are a veteran who remembers the clinking of coins in arcade halls or a younger gamer curious about the roots of your favorite hobby, this museum is an absolute must-visit in Zagreb. I cannot recommend it highly enough!

reviews.author: Tatya Layung Nareswari
reviews.text: Had such a great time at the Video Game History Museum! The collection is fantastic, from early computers and consoles to iconic handhelds and arcade machines, all thoughtfully displayed. The best part is that you can actually play on almost all of them, which makes the experience super interactive and way more fun than just looking at displays. I also loved how each room is recreated by decade, it brought back so much nostalgia, especially the 2000s room. The museum isn’t very big, but it’s packed with things to try and explore, and it never feels boring. Definitely worth a visit if you love games or grew up with them.

reviews.author: Sharon M Alfonso
reviews.text:
It was amazing.
I loved the game world and when I found a place where you can understand its story and evolution. Besides, you can play there!
It was a fun and a memorable visit!

reviews.author: MARIJA JAKOVLJEVIĆ
reviews.text: I celebrated my birthday with my friends at the museum for the first time. A wonderful experience! First, we had an interesting and exciting praline-making workshop (for adults). The hostess and host Ružica was a wonderful hostess, warm and kind and above all patient with us. After that, we had a tour of the museum with the guide Domagoj. Lots of interesting facts about chocolate with a chocolate tasting. The guide's stories and the exhibition program with examples and various samples, as well as the museum's display itself, keep visitors interested until the end of the tour. On the way out, we received our pralines that we had prepared. A must-see if you're in Zagreb, especially for chocolate lovers. Enjoy it as much as we did. Kudos to the museum manager for the organization and display in the museum. Great !!!

reviews.author: panagiota georgiou
reviews.text:
If you’re a true chocolate lover, the Chocolate Museum in Zagreb is an experience you simply cannot miss. From the moment you enter, you’re immersed in a world of chocolate aromas and sweet delights that tempt you to try everything.

One of the highlights for me was a small “box” filled with chocolate pieces that show the evolution of chocolate over time. Each piece tells its own story and flavor, letting you see how chocolate transformed from a simple treat into a luxurious delight.

At the end of the tour, there are three chocolate fountains—white, milk, and dark—where you can dip as much as you want. This part of the experience allows you to experiment, taste different combinations, and learn about the varieties and textures of chocolate in a fun and interactive way.

Overall, it’s a journey full of sweet discoveries, learning, and pure indulgence for any chocolate enthusiast. Even if you think you know chocolate well, this museum will surprise and delight you.

reviews.author: Gary Brazel
reviews.text:
It's interesting to learn all about chocolate. The signage is both in Croatian and English.
You get a box with some samples (nibs, white chocolate, dark, etc.) When you reach where they discuss that type you then eat them.
At the end are three fountains where you can dispense the liquid samples onto the wooden spoon provided for you in the sample box. I didn't find these liquids as tasty as the solid samples in the box.

reviews.author: Signe Homčenko
reviews.text:
I really loved it! I like how going through the museum you get to taste pieces of chocholate and see how it evolved from quite bitter chocholate to different kinds we have today!
Very cute displays and I liked to see the different packagings and dishes that were used and how everything worked years ago.
I truly suggest visiting this place🫶🏼

reviews.author: Yun Huang
reviews.text: very creative way to engage - give you different chocolates to taste that followed what was introduced in different periods of time! Learned a lot of chocolate! It will be better if there are more products to purchase, we cannot find any product there we want, which is abit disappointing

reviews.author: Camille F.
reviews.text: The most inspiring and creative space since art school! Amazing atmosphere and concept, you have the opportunity to try out different types of art. I felt very much in the flow while working on little projects. I wish every city had such a place, loved it!

reviews.author: Dorothea Degenhart
reviews.text: This place truely is so unique! It is like an art studio where you can try out everything you want! We wouldn’t wanna leave and stayed there for hours! The owner also is such a kind soul, we had some really interesting conversations. It’s definitely worth a visit and really worth the money! Thank you for this highlight of our vacation in Zagreb. ☺️

reviews.author: Tami K.
reviews.text: We spend beautiful hours here, creating wonderful own art, working on unfinished participatory projects and learning so much about different techniques. The owner is really lovely and we had a really nice chat. The whole place is created with so much love and an eye for esthetics, the vintage furniture perfectly fits this really lovely concept. It's absoluty worth spending time here. Thank you so much for this experience.

reviews.author: Jelica Dojčinović
reviews.text: In this museum you make art. Engage with everything you see, try different art techniques or learn how to make embroidery or stamps. For kids but also for adults, you can spend hours there as it’s pleasant and cozy place where you can be creative and enjoy in making something artsy. The host will be there to show you everything and she is so nice lady. We had a great time and we’ll come back.

reviews.author: Natalie Wong
reviews.text:
This is a wonderful museum for families to visit. There are many different crafts and creative activities for both adults and kids to enjoy like weaving, sand art and type-writing and more.  We stayed for nearly two hours and left feeling truly happy.

I can't imagine how deeply one can focus on art and creativity here—especially in a time everyone is so used to staring at our phones all day. It was a refreshing and meaningful experience for not only children but also for adults.

The curator (owner) is a very friendly lady, fluent in English, who generously shared her thoughts on art and education with us.

The museum is really great!  It gave us a joyful Saturday morning and deeply inspired our children's creativity and imagination. Highly recommended for anyone visiting Zagreb!

reviews.author: Marissa Licata
reviews.text: Friggin LOVED this place. I thought it was going to be dumb but ended up being my favorite museum of Zagreb! I had a great time taking photos. All the exhibits are fun and cute. Definitely worth a trip.

reviews.author: Satori ...
reviews.text: A fun stop to take great pictures in specially designed environments. The woman working was very kind and assisted us with our pictures!

reviews.author: Rebekah C
reviews.text: So fun!!! It’s such a random thing to do in Zagreb, but we had a good time! Plan to spend roughly an hour there if you do all the photo spots.

reviews.author: sandy roo
reviews.text: My sister and I (grown adults!) had so much fun here, running around between the "sets" waving props around and having a good laugh. Photos came out well, and are fun to share. Overall, better than expected!

reviews.author: K R
reviews.text: My visit to the Selfie Museum Zagreb was an incredibly fun and unique experience. The interactive exhibits allowed me to explore different themed rooms and capture amazing photos. The staff was friendly and helpful, making my visit even more enjoyable.

reviews.author: Mia Ceponja
reviews.text:
Magnificent museum, amazing tour guide and all the team there.
We really enjoyed It. The whole building is such a diamond location where it is based in the Upper town.   Truly amazing.

reviews.author: Hrvoje Petrovic
reviews.text:
Katarina Velika fantastična izložba, sa lijepim primjercima umjetnina iz Ermitraža St. Petersburga koje će se teško u ovolikom obimu moći ubuduće vidjeti u ovim krajevima. Cijena ulaznice je 60kn, i treba oko 2 sata da se sve vidi i na miru poslika.😎

Katarina the Great - fantastic museum show, with a lot of interesting art pieces from St. Petersburg Ermitrage which will be hard to see in such amount in upcoming years in this area. Ticket is 60HRK you would need 2 hours for easy going exploration.

reviews.author: Reinaldo Gabay
reviews.text: Large building, old monastery, located in the Gornji Grad. Excellent exhibitions, they usually take place on the first and second floors. Amazing views of Zagreb's Cathedral from the inside. The place also has a store with variety of books, posters and other materials.

reviews.author: G f
reviews.text:
A great museum with a rotating exhibit, currently about really cool robots!
The staff is very friendly and happy when you come in. I only speak English and they were very friendly and spoke perfectly English to help guide me. They even gave me directions to another shop so I can find certain gifts friends have been asking me for.

reviews.author: Tomislav Kralj
reviews.text:
🟡An okay museum.
Been there for two exhibits, for Bodies and World of Robots, and for some reason, both exhibits were very bland. It's a large space, so lots of exhibits can be shown, but every time I went there the rooms were half empty.
Not sure why that is. I probably won't go there a third time.
✅️ The Staff is friendly and capable.
💲💲Prices are ok. Expensive, but not too expensive.
⚠️ This is the center of Zagreb so NO PARKING nearby. Your best bet is to park somewhere else like Garaža Tuškanac and walk to the museum.
⚠️ If you see some exhibit that you REALLY REALLY like, then visit. Otherwise, avoid.

reviews.author: Richard Sharp
reviews.text: I loved it. It's not the largest technical museum or the most complete (in fact the array of topics covered seems a little bizarre) but the exhibits it has, for the subjects it covers, are superb. Well worth a visit if you happen to be in Zagreb and have the time.

reviews.author: Nas Sol
reviews.text: A very impressive and comprehensive museum. I could have spent much more time as there is so much to take in. The museum Architecure to begin with is beautiful and once you enter everything is arranged really well. Incredible engineering marvels here and lovely to see the motor and train displays. I was amazed with what it has to offer.

reviews.author: Νάνσυ Σιγάλα
reviews.text:
An outstanding experience and a perfect example of how a museum should treat its visitors.
We arrived only 30 minutes before closing, and not only were we warmly welcomed, but we were also allowed to visit the museum free of charge due to the late hour, something truly rare and deeply appreciated.
The staff was exceptionally kind, helpful, and professional. They took the time to explain how to move through the museum efficiently, as the museum is quite large, so that we could manage to see as much as possible within the limited time.
Everything was very well maintained, with clear English descriptions throughout. The exhibits are presented in a structured way, offering historical context and practical explanations of the inventions, devices, and scientific principles behind them. Even in a short visit, it felt informative and easy to follow.
The museum also hosts live demonstrations/experiments (including Tesla-related ones) unfortunately, we didn’t have time to catch one, which gives us one more reason to come back.
A truly inspiring and well-organized museum with wonderful staff.
We will definitely visit again and highly recommend it.

reviews.author: Kevin Costello
reviews.text: Fascinating museum which covers a broad range of technology and innovation and shows the progressive sophistication of each genre. I also went on a mine tour which involved walking through various types of pit shafts and saw a great talk about Tesla and his contributions with some spectacular special effects. At 6 euros this museum represents fantastic value and offers a high end experience in Zagreb. Awesome!!!

reviews.author: Gary Brazel
reviews.text:
Although he did most of his work in the United States, he was born and grew up in Croatia so it's fitting that there is a museum in his name in Zagreb.
It's collection of technology is wide ranging- automobiles, spaceship and rocket models, fire fighting equipment, sewing machines,  planes, a submarine, etc. While the museum is worth visiting, there are some negatives:
They should have the Testla coil demonstration more often. We were there at 11:00 and there wasn't going to be one until 3:30 PM. The planetarium wasn't open until 4:00.
The Foucault pendulum should be in motion but it wasn't.

places.name: Travel Experience Museum / Muzej putovanja
places.address: Ilica 1 Prolaz Neboder / Neboder Passage, 10000, Donji Grad, Zagreb, Croatia

reviews.author: Antonia Jagodić
reviews.text: Amazing experience and very interesting museum. It is in the center of Zagreb. With your ticket you can make photos and later have a postcard with photo at destination you want. It is very interactive for kids also with plenty of spots with VR glasses that lead you in amazing places, exotic landscapes, deserts and rainforests.

reviews.author: Anita Cvetnić
reviews.text: Great Museum, great tour with prof. D.Nemčić and TV host L.Bulić. We Watch 3D Titanic and all was wonderfull ❤️Thank you

reviews.author: Lara Medic
reviews.text: Right in the very center of Zagreb! Interesting to visit and relax, especially in the large rooms with projections.

reviews.author: Rhiannon Deveraux
reviews.text: The concept is great, really good idea. But for £11 per person I was expecting more!! It's very very small. Also if it got busy I could imagine not enjoying it at all, you'd have to wait for ages to use the VR headsets and it would feel very cramped. I personally wouldn't recommend paying £11 per adult for it.

reviews.author: Balkancierge
reviews.text: Basically, it's a tourist trap - just like the museum of chocolate franchise, the 'selfie' museum, museum of hangovers. None of the exhibits have anything to do with the cultural heritage of Zagreb or Croatia. Likely put up as a franchise or by EU Funds being clearly misused for commercial enterprises. You'll better spend your money simply walking around the city for a true travel experience.

reviews.author: Joaquín Fernández
reviews.text:
A small but really nice place full of new wave rock (also rap, hip-hop, etc) history of the country and it's bands, not only that, it has a lot of artifacts and documents that let you imagine how the scene was at the time and how the culture evolved.
I was able to talk to the owners, really nice people, on the history of the museum and their personal experiences.
Cheers from Uruguay!

reviews.author: Stefan Jovanović
reviews.text: Cool and interesting place. You can read and see stories about  the new wave music scene in 1970s and 1980s Zagreb and feel the unique atmosphere of it. Our host and curator (and owner probably) of the museum was a very friendly gentleman who shared some interesting stories and told us about newer events in our region of former Yugoslavia.

reviews.author: Vlad Țepeș
reviews.text:
Thank you Tomislav for our conversation and the vinyl recommandation for my parents !
I Hope one day you can open this big museum talking not only about Zagreb bands's new wave but from all of Yugoslavia !
Vidimo se u Zagrebu!

reviews.author: Hasitha Subasinghe
reviews.text: Its like something from the 90ies! There are all sorts of events. A great venue. The owner is great. Probably needs some refurbishment. However its charming and nice.

reviews.author: Felipe Fernández
reviews.text:
Very small museum depicting the new wave movement in Croatia and Yugoslavia. Not much to see, but a lot of interesting stuff to read if you're into music and would like to discover bands you may have never heard of if you're not from the region.
Do not skip talking to the owner and host, he was extremely nice and was able to tell us about the Yugoslavusic scene from his own experience

reviews.author: Toma Bulić
reviews.text:
The place is great, I've had a lot of fun and there is plenty of things to explore.
The whole vibe is great and funny, and the staff is great. The tickets are relatively cheap. Would recommend a visit if you are in Zagreb.
A bit crowded but ok.

reviews.author: Lone Traveler
reviews.text: I went by chance as i was solo on bussiness trip in Zagreb.First, lady at reception desk i have some jokes about ticket ,she was very friendly and explained to me that i can go in by stairs or by slide tunel 😁.Amazing things to see and learn something new so next time i can bring my daughter with me as i am sure she will like it.If you want to have some fun with your kids or friends all reccomendation to everyone to go to see this amazing place.

reviews.author: Isabella Bastos
reviews.text:
I had such a great time at the Ha Ha Review! The atmosphere was super welcoming, the comedians were hilarious, and the lineup had a good mix of styles and personalities. It felt authentic, fun, and very engaging. definitely one of those experiences that makes you feel the local vibe while still being easy to enjoy as a visitor. The venue was cozy, the staff were friendly, and the crowd’s energy made the night even better.

If you’re in Zagreb and want to do something different from the usual sightseeing, I 100% recommend checking this out. It’s the perfect way to spend an evening, laugh a lot, and meet great people. ❤️❤️❤️❤️

reviews.author: Leah Gidberg
reviews.text:
The HaHaHouse was the perfect way for spending our last day in Zagreb!
From the very first moment of entering the museum you start laughing and finish about anhour after you leave 😄
The attractions are all interactive and leave the visitors with fun memories, the staff is very friendly and the location is very easy to reach. I definitely recommend!

reviews.author: Mateja Mihaljević
reviews.text: We recently visited the Museum of Laughter with our six-year-old. The concept is fun and the exhibits are definitely interactive, which is great for kids but be prepared to wait your turn, as they let a lot of people in at once. Some of the displays were entertaining, though others felt a bit sparse. Overall, it was a so-so experience: enjoyable in parts, but the crowds and waiting took away from the fun. Good for a quick visit, especially with little ones, but don’t expect a fully immersive adventure.

places.name: Ethnography Museum
places.address: Trg Antuna, Ivana i Vladimira Mažuranića 14, 10000, Zagreb, Croatia

reviews.author: Kikimora_Taste_Nomad
reviews.text: There are currently four different exhibitions on display. The ticket price is €4.

reviews.author: Katrina Nicolas
reviews.text: The museum is quite impressive, and we had the chance to explore the traditional clothing and fabrics from various regions of Croatia. They also feature an exhibition highlighting items that some Croatians have brought back from other countries.

reviews.author: Renata Rogic
reviews.text: What a wonderful little gem! I enjoyed every moment! Croatian traditional clothes was just unbelievable. I am surprised this museum is not more popular. Even though it is quite small, it is well worth the visit.

reviews.author: Lori McAfee
reviews.text: Small but very nice with a collection of Croatian costume, furniture, and excellent stories.  I enjoyed learning more about life and history of Croatia. The additional displays of artifacts from other countries were excellent also, and the photos from the early 1900s of the families living in the train cars.

reviews.author: RobertsRoamers
reviews.text:
We really enjoyed our time here , there are so many wonderful and interesting things about Croatia here from the national dress to how they live
Also some tv screens explaining about how people live with subtitles in English
There was also an interesting exhibition about intimate space with how and where  these people feel happiest and how they created the space from , memories and interesting pieces of furniture
We spent about just over one  hour exploring this museum, so give your self time to see it all
It’s only €5 entrance fee and we were the only ones there

reviews.author: Hamy Nguyen
reviews.text: cute little museum with fairly priced entrance fee of 5€ (for students 3.50€). there was a lot of informative texts to learn from and there were also surveys that you can fill in for the museum, which i found very cool since this is a good way for visitors to communicate with the museum even though it’s indirectly.

reviews.author: Signe Homčenko
reviews.text: It was not my cup of tea, but i can appreciate the dainty work that has been put in the paintings and how effective they look painted on the glass. If you are a lover of art then it is a must see! 😊

reviews.author: Gary Brazel
reviews.text: Nice little museum originally known as the Peasant Art Museum). It was founded in 1952.

reviews.author: Ksenia Shestakovskaia
reviews.text: Fantastic little museum for folk and naive art lovers. Small but so exiting! Lovely people working there. Just to see works of Ivan Rabuzin was worth  losing the breath climbing. Extraordinary!

reviews.author: Jakob Turnup
reviews.text: I visited this little museum during my trip to Zagreb the last weekend. The exhibition contained a lot of different sceneries. The ticket price was very fair.

places.name: Kuća karikature Oto Reisinger
places.address: Na vrhu Mlinskih stuba, Ul. Pavla Radića 44A, 10000, Zagreb, Croatia

reviews.author: Sofiya Öztekin
reviews.text:
If you’re in Zagreb, this is definitely a place you should visit. This unique legacy, brought to life through the tireless efforts of Oto Reisinger’s family in his memory, is truly worth experiencing. The exhibition offers a fascinating glimpse into the world of one of Croatia’s most beloved cartoonists, celebrating his humor, creativity, and sharp social observations.

Inside, you’ll find hundreds of original cartoons, books, sketches, and newspaper clippings that showcase his long and influential career. The atmosphere is both nostalgic and inspiring, making it enjoyable not only for art lovers but for anyone who appreciates clever, thoughtful humor. Thanks to the Croatian-to-English translations provided throughout the exhibition, international visitors can fully understand and appreciate the wit and messages behind his work.

Whether you spend a quick hour or take your time exploring every detail, you’re guaranteed to leave with a smile and a deeper appreciation for the art of cartooning. It’s a warm, charming cultural stop that adds something truly special to your time in Zagreb.

reviews.author: Anita Miočić-Stošić
reviews.text:
If you’re visiting Zagreb, don’t miss the House of Caricature dedicated to the legendary Otto Reisinger. This charming little museum is a true hidden gem – beautifully designed, thoughtfully curated, and filled with an impressive collection of original works and memorabilia.
Otto Reisinger was a world-class artist, and the exhibition does a wonderful job of celebrating both his talent and his legacy. What makes the experience even more special is the personal touch: the museum was founded by his grandson Marko as a heartfelt tribute to his famous grandfather.
Marko is incredibly kind, welcoming, and engaging. His passion for his grandfather’s work is contagious, and he adds depth and warmth to the visit.
This place is more than a museum – it’s a small oasis of humor, creativity, and lightness in the heart of Zagreb. Highly recommended for anyone who appreciates art and wit.

reviews.author: 黃馨賢
reviews.text: This is a hidden gem in Zagreb. We didn’t know Oto Reisinger at first, but after visiting, we found his cartoons absolutely brilliant — witty, humorous, and ironic. We’re really glad we decided to visit this museum.

reviews.author: awanafan
reviews.text: I discovered this gem while organizing my trip, and it truly exceeded my expectations. The artist's work is remarkable. I visited on a rainy day, which meant the museum was quiet, allowing me to fully immerse myself in the experience. I found the art to be both entertaining and humorous. The layout of the exhibits was thoughtfully designed and easy to navigate. The receptionist was delightful; she provided excellent care and offered valuable advice. Her kindness was truly appreciated.

reviews.author: Biggi m
reviews.text:
We had a great time visiting this museum during our stay in Zagreb.
The cartoons were very funny and understandable without a lot of cultural/historical background. It gave a glimpse of Croatian mentality and history though.
Further more the owner was very and helpful. The price 7 € for students (8€ for adults) was reasonable and well worth it. You can easily spend 1 or 2 hours.
Also the view on the old town is quite nice.

Can only recommend!

reviews.author: Sakina Soukaina
reviews.text:
Great view of Zagreb from this tower.
The entrance fee is 3€ but it’s worth it.
You have to go up about 4 floors and some areas are steep but it’s doable.
I recommend especially if it’s a good day.

reviews.author: Сева
reviews.text: If you go outside of tourist season, you can hang out with the cannoneer while he fires the gun. Pretty awesome experience. Great view from the top too. And friendly folks at the information desk!

reviews.author: Ramona H.
reviews.text:
If you go to the top you will get a nice view over Zagreb. Every day at 12 am they will shoot the cannon.

The entry was EUR 3.

reviews.author: Varsha Degwekar
reviews.text:
The entry ticket of €6 for the watch tower entrance was totally worth it.
The spiral staircase was narrow but well maintained. The steep 116 steps climb was daunting at first but there were two landings.
It was a brilliant day the views were spectacular

reviews.author: negin ghaderi
reviews.text: It was nice, with a view of the town. Don’t expect something very tall or a large place. They shoot every day at 12. It was cool to be there at 12, as all tourists gather together and count down. Overall all a must-visit place in Zagreb if you ask me.👍

reviews.author: Mohil Kapoor
reviews.text: The whole area has a different vibe altogether. Classic style architecture. Restaurants opposite and lot of people around

reviews.author: Ramona H.
reviews.text: This is a beautiful cathedrale. On the way to the entrance there are some panels with information about the construction of the building (at least I think so). Unfortunately the panel were in Croation so I didn't understand them.

reviews.author: Svetlana Margetová
reviews.text:
During our Christmas walk through Zagreb with Ondra, Brano, and Rolko (Roland), we visited Zagreb Cathedral, and despite the circumstances, it was still an impressive experience. The cathedral is absolutely massive and towers above the city - its twin spires reaching 108 meters make it Croatia's tallest building, and you can see it from almost everywhere in Zagreb!

When we visited, the cathedral was completely wrapped in scaffolding - which was a bit disappointing since we couldn't see the full Gothic architecture - but even so, it remained beautiful and imposing. The sheer scale of the structure is breathtaking, and you can still appreciate its grandeur despite the ongoing restoration work. Unfortunately, the cathedral was closed to visitors during our visit, which was sad because we would have loved to see the interior with its famous sacristy, stained glass windows, and the treasury.

What made our visit special was the foggy weather - the cathedral emerging from the mist with all that scaffolding created its own unique, almost mysterious atmosphere. It felt both historic and contemporary at the same time. There's also a stopped clock outside that permanently shows 7:03 - the exact time when the devastating 1880 earthquake struck Zagreb, which is a powerful historical detail.

Interesting historical fact: Zagreb Cathedral has an incredibly dramatic history of destruction and rebirth! The original cathedral from 1217 was destroyed by the Mongols in 1242, then rebuilt. After surviving Ottoman invasions (fortification walls were built around it, some still visible today), it was devastated again by a massive 6.3 magnitude earthquake on November 9, 1880, which collapsed the main nave. Architect Hermann Bollé then rebuilt it in the stunning neo-Gothic style you see today (1880-1902). But here's the remarkable part: renovation work began in December 1990 and the cathedral has been continuously covered in scaffolding for over 30 years - meaning an estimated 60% of Zagreb's current population has never seen the towers without scaffolding! Then in March 2020, another earthquake struck, causing a 10.3-meter section of the south tower to collapse. Now the cathedral is undergoing another major restoration with over 600 tons of steel scaffolding to protect it from future earthquakes. The resilience of this cathedral mirrors the resilience of Zagreb itself!

A must-see landmark even with scaffolding - hopefully one day we can return when it's fully restored!

reviews.author: Balkrishna Shroff
reviews.text: It is a great piece of architecture just 100 meters from main square. Inside it is very beautiful. It has a small connection with Las Vegas. A Croatian owner of a casino in Las Vegas had gifted them 3 chandeliers which are prominently displayed though they look little out of place. The atmosphere inside was very calm and soothing. It is better to see with a guide who can explain the great historical background. Worth seeing !!

reviews.author: Adriano
reviews.text:
I was here on the 8/12/2025 and it was covered in scaffoldings. It has been consecutively covered in them since the eighties and there is not an end in sight. I honestly think it’s frankly disrespectful to deprive citizens and tourists of such an important religious and cultural landmark in Croatia. Why can’t they just finish the work???
Beside the frustrating situation the church is breathing even if it’s barely even visible. You cannot even get inside and take a look at the interior design. Even the square in front of it it’s under renovation… it’s almost comical. Such a shame it’s our reality

reviews.author: 張凱程
reviews.text: Stone Gate (Kamenita Vrata) is a small but deeply moving place in the heart of Zagreb’s old town. Walking through the historic gate feels like stepping back in time, and the shrine inside is peaceful and full of warm candlelight. The ironwork details and the many plaques of gratitude create a powerful atmosphere of faith and history. It’s open 24 hours, easy to visit, and perfect for a quiet moment during your walk through the city. Highly recommended.

reviews.author: Guillermo Flores
reviews.text: A really nice local spot. If you’re into history or religion , this is a nice spot to come to. There’s a little store next to the Virgin Mary painting where you can buy candles. I also recommend a souvenir store just passed the gate. A very nice husband and wife operate the store as seen in my picture.

reviews.author: Hajar Belayate
reviews.text: It is an unusual cathedral, more like a passage than a typical church. The guide told us that there was a fire in the past, and only one painting of the Virgin Mary was not damaged. It is definitely a must-see place.

reviews.author: Svetlana Margetová
reviews.text:
During our Christmas walk through Zagreb with Ondra, Brano, and Tomi, we passed through the Stone Gate – one of the most special and atmospheric places in the entire Upper Town. This is honestly such a unique historical monument because it's not just a gate, it has this incredibly strong religious feeling that you can sense the moment you walk into the passageway.

The Stone Gate itself is beautiful and historic – it's the only remaining medieval gate from the original fortifications of Zagreb, believed to have been built around 1266. But what makes it truly special is the shrine inside the passageway. As you walk through, you'll see the chapel on one side with its gilded icon of the Virgin Mary holding baby Jesus, surrounded by flickering candlelight. The atmosphere is so peaceful and spiritual, even as you're just passing through as a pedestrian.

What really caught my attention were all the written things on the walls – the southern and eastern walls are covered with marble plaques engraved with prayers and messages of thanks. Most of them say "Hvala ti" which means "Thank you" in Croatian. It's touching to see how many people have left these messages of gratitude over the years. The whole space feels like a living, breathing part of Zagreb's spiritual life, not just a tourist attraction.

The religious significance of this gate comes from one of the most miraculous events in Zagreb's history! On May 31, 1731, a devastating fire swept through Gradec (the medieval Upper Town), destroying most of the wooden structures and houses. The Stone Gate was engulfed in flames, and everything burned – except for one thing: the painting of the Virgin Mary and Child that was displayed in the gate. When residents sifted through the ashes, they found the painting completely unharmed, with only its wooden frame destroyed! This was immediately seen as a divine miracle, and a widow named Modlar who had lost everything in the fire set up a small altar in the gate's passageway so everyone could come and venerate the holy image. In 1929, experts confirmed that the painting had indeed been touched by flames that destroyed its wooden backing, yet the image itself remained intact – despite not being painted on any fire-resistant material! This miracle transformed the Stone Gate into Zagreb's biggest shrine. The tradition of praying here dates back to medieval times when townsfolk would pray to Mary for safety every time they went to work on farms outside the gate, then give thanks on their way home. On May 31, 1991 (the 260th anniversary of the miracle), Cardinal Franjo Kuharić officially declared Our Lady of the Stone Gate the patron saint of Zagreb, and May 31st is now celebrated as the Day of the City! Even today, locals come every day to light candles and pray, and every Tuesday at 8 PM, people gather here to pray the rosary together. It's said that even the pavement under the arch is still the original from centuries ago!

A truly remarkable place where history, faith, and daily life intersect in the heart of Zagreb!

reviews.author: Alvaro Lau
reviews.text: Is one of Zagreb’s most historic landmarks and an important spiritual place for locals. Walking through the old gate feels like stepping back in time. Many people stop to light a candle or say a prayer, making it both a cultural and religious symbol of the city. A must-see when exploring the Upper Town.

reviews.author: Hajar Belayate
reviews.text: Tunel Grič is surprisingly beautiful and atmospheric. The walls and arches give it a unique charm, and the lighting highlights its historic character. I was so surprised because I thought it would be small, but it is actually very long. Walking through the tunnel feels like stepping back in time. It’s a hidden gem in Zagreb that’s definitely worth seeing.

reviews.author: Tomasz Nguyen xuan
reviews.text:
The highlight of our visit to the downtown of Zagreb. There are beautiful lights and sounds asking the the tunnel, so the walk makes it a very special event. Don't hesitate to come here if you're visiting Zagreb.

There's a parking lot nearby if you are coming by car (Tuskanac). However, mind that the traffic is terrible and takes a lot of time to get to the city centre from anywhere around.

reviews.author: A G
reviews.text:
Grič Tunnel – Zagreb’s surprising underground art gallery (May 7, 2025)
This long, arched WWII air-raid shelter beneath the Upper Town has been transformed into one of Zagreb’s coolest hidden gems. Walking through the cool, dimly lit 350-meter concrete tunnel feels like stepping into a secret world—especially when it’s filled with bright, joyful art exhibitions like the one we saw: children’s paintings, colorful canvases, hanging sculptures, and handmade pieces lining the walls and ceiling, turning the old bunker into a vibrant underground gallery. The contrast between the raw wartime history and the lively creativity is magical. It’s free, quick to walk through (connects Mesnička and Radićeva streets), and a perfect rainy-day or anytime discovery in Zagreb. Don’t miss it—pure unexpected wonder!

reviews.author: Balkrishna Shroff
reviews.text: It is one of the most important tourist spot to visit in Zagreb. Please note Zagreb is a historical place so it is better to hire a guide and understand their history. If you visit in summer like we did then you will be shocked to see the change in temperature once you enter it. It was very cool inside. It's amazing.

reviews.author: Svetlana Margetová
reviews.text:
During our Christmas walk through Zagreb with Ondra, Rolko, and Brano, we walked through the Grič Tunnel. We'd heard there was some kind of Christmas installation inside, but honestly, we found it a bit disappointing – not as impressive as we expected based on what people were saying. The "Polar Dream" installation with hanging fabrics and colored lights was okay, but felt a bit sparse and underwhelming for such a hyped attraction.

What made things worse was that we didn't even know what this tunnel actually was or why it existed! There wasn't much information posted inside about its history, so we just walked through thinking it was some random pedestrian passage. Only later did I learn about its fascinating backstory. On the plus side, the tunnel was definitely warmer than being outside in the cold December weather, so it provided a nice break from the winter chill – at least that was practical!

The tunnel itself is about 350 meters long and connects Mesnička Street to Radićeva Street, cutting right through the Grič hill beneath the Upper Town. It's a convenient shortcut if you're navigating the city center, and the walk through takes about 5 minutes. The entrance is marked with the Zagreb coat of arms. During non-Advent times, it's just a simple pedestrian walkway, though it does host various cultural events, exhibitions, and even wine festivals throughout the year.

Grič Tunnel has an absolutely fascinating history that I wish we'd known about while walking through it! It was built in 1943 during World War II as a bomb shelter to protect civilians from Allied air raids. The project was ordered by the prime minister of the Independent State of Croatia and was supposed to take just 90 days to build, but the construction was plagued by criticism and cost overruns – the budget exploded from 141 million kuna to 490 million kuna! The tunnel was finished in 1944 and actually served its intended purpose, providing shelter during bombings. After WWII, the tunnel fell into disuse and became occupied by homeless people and drug users for decades. Then, during the Croatian War of Independence in the 1990s, it served as a bomb shelter once again! But here's the coolest part: in 1993, right in the middle of the war, the tunnel became the site of Croatia's first major rave party called "Under City Rave" with over 1,000 people attending, featuring DJs from Germany and the UK – even MTV came to cover it! People used it as a symbol of resistance and a desire for peace and music during the war. The tunnel remained closed and neglected until finally being renovated and reopened to the public in July 2016. The Zagreb coat of arms that marks the tunnel entrances has special symbolism – if you look at the coat of arms on St. Mark's Church roof, you'll see a door beneath the castle, which according to legend represents the entrance to secret underground passages that supposedly run throughout Zagreb's Upper Town! The legend says these tunnels connect to the Croatian Parliament building and even to Medvedgrad Castle on Medvednica mountain, though most passages were allegedly destroyed in the great 1880 earthquake along with hidden church gold!

Worth a quick walk-through, especially if you want to escape the cold or avoid the busy streets above, though I'd recommend reading up on its history first to appreciate it more!

reviews.author: Seokjin Ham
reviews.text: This elegant Art Nouveau passage connects Petar Preradović Square with Ilica Street. It was originally designed by Josip Vancaš between 1898 and 1900 as part of the First Croatian Savings Bank building. The passage is best known for its central octagonal atrium topped with a beautiful glass dome. Today, it serves as a shopping arcade, home to various shops and boutiques, where visitors can enjoy a unique blend of architectural beauty and retail experience.

reviews.author: Latoyaa Roberts
reviews.text: Cool nice gallerie are with some stores and there was a nice display of a christmas tree using words from a song.

reviews.author: Guilherme Cavalcante

reviews.author: Svetlana Margetová
reviews.text:
During our Christmas walk through Zagreb Advent with Ondra, Rolko (Roland), and Brano, we came across this fascinating attraction - "Zagreb Welcomes You" - a beautiful bronze scale model of the city. It's located in the open area between Ban Jelačić Square and Kaptol, making it easy to spot during your city exploration.

The model itself is impressive in its detail and craftsmanship. It's a 3.65 meter long and 3.55 meter wide bronze sculpture that represents 2.64 square kilometers of the urban area. What amazed us was the incredible detail - it features 2,515 addresses, 153 city blocks, 19 squares, 128 streets, and 421 structures, all beautifully cast in bronze! You can see all the important landmarks and tourist attractions of Zagreb's city center in miniature form.

The model was sculpted by Professor Damir Mataušić, and it's become more than just a tourist attraction - it's actually replaced the famous clock on Ban Jelačić Square as a popular meeting point for locals! Along the base, there are welcome messages written in 38 languages and texts about Zagreb's history in both Croatian and English.

We have similar city models in our cities too (like Langweil's Model in Prague), so it was nice to see Zagreb's version. It's a great way to get an overview of the city's layout and identify the main landmarks before exploring them in person.

Interesting historical fact: City scale models have a fascinating history! Prague's famous Langweil's Model, created between 1826-1837 by university library worker Antonín Langweil, took 11 years to complete and covers 20 square meters showing over 2,500 buildings at 1:480 scale. Langweil got the inspiration after seeing a 3D model of Paris exhibited in Prague! These models weren't just tourist attractions - they served as important historical documentation of cities. Zagreb's model, unveiled on June 20th, continues this tradition while serving as both an artistic sculpture and a practical orientation tool for visitors. The interactive element makes it perfect for understanding the city's geography - you can literally touch history and see how the medieval Kaptol (where the cathedral is) connects to the modern city center!

A nice little attraction to pause at while exploring Zagreb's Advent markets!

reviews.author: Cláudio Cardona
reviews.text:
The “Zagreb Welcomes You” model is a captivating miniature representation of the city, offering visitors a unique and detailed perspective of Zagreb. This scale model allows for an appreciation of the city’s layout and architectural beauty in a way that walking the streets cannot provide.

Detailed Representation

The model meticulously showcases Zagreb’s urban landscape, including its iconic buildings, streets, and public squares. The attention to detail in this model is remarkable, offering insights into the city’s architectural diversity and urban planning. For visitors new to the city, it serves as an excellent introduction to the layout and key landmarks of Zagreb.

Educational Value

“Zagreb Welcomes You” is more than just a visual treat; it’s an educational experience. It provides a comprehensive overview of the city’s structure, making it easier for visitors to understand the city’s geography and plan their explorations. It’s particularly beneficial for those interested in architecture and urban design.

Interactive Experience

Visiting the model can be an interactive experience, as it allows you to visualize your routes and identify places of interest before or after exploring the city. It serves as a practical tool for planning your visit and ensures you don’t miss out on any of Zagreb’s key attractions.

Overall Impression

The “Zagreb Welcomes You” model is a must-see for anyone visiting the city. It’s not only a beautiful representation of Zagreb but also an informative and practical guide to understanding and appreciating the city’s rich urban tapestry. This model provides a unique perspective that complements the experience of exploring Zagreb on foot.

reviews.author: A G
reviews.text:
Mark’s Church – Zagreb’s iconic tiled-roof masterpiece (May 7, 2025)
Even under construction scaffolding and barriers, St. Mark’s Church remains one of the most instantly recognizable buildings in Croatia. Perched proudly in the heart of the Upper Town (Gornji Grad) on St. Mark’s Square, this 13th-century Gothic church is famous worldwide for its brilliantly colored tiled roof, which features the massive coats of arms of medieval Croatia, Dalmatia, Slavonia, and the city of Zagreb itself – a dazzling patchwork of red, white, blue, and gold that looks like a giant heraldic painting against the sky.
The south portal with its 15th-century sculptures of the Virgin Mary, St. Mark, and other saints is a beautiful example of Gothic stone carving, and the simple interior (when open) feels peaceful and timeless. On our visit, the ongoing restoration work couldn’t hide the magic – the tiled roof still peeked out dramatically, the square buzzed with visitors taking photos, and the whole scene felt like a living postcard of Zagreb’s medieval soul. A must-see landmark, even mid-renovation – it’s impossible not to fall in love with this colorful piece of history! 🏰🇭🇷

reviews.author: Petr Novák
reviews.text:
1 | St. Mark’s Church in Zagreb dates back to the 13th century, making it one of the city’s oldest architectural monuments. It is easily recognized by its vividly tiled roof, which displays the coats of arms of the medieval Triune Kingdom of Croatia‑Slavonia‑Dalmatia and the City of Zagreb.
2 | The church roof is very photogenic, and the site is worth a visit. During our stay in Zagreb in July 2025, the church was under renovation, and construction container towers were set up in front of it. The interior couldn't be visited, and it was nearly impossible to take a decent photo of the church.

reviews.author: Svetlana Margetová
reviews.text:
We visited this beautiful Christmas market during our Zagreb trip with Ondra, Roland, and Brano, and I have mixed feelings about the experience. On one hand, the atmosphere, decorations, and overall vibe were absolutely magical - everything you could want from a European Christmas market. On the other hand, it was so incredibly crowded that it was sometimes difficult to fully enjoy.

The setting in Zrinjevac Park itself is stunning. The entire park is transformed into a winter wonderland with thousands of sparkling lights illuminating the century-old plane trees, creating an enchanting canopy of twinkling lights overhead. The colors and festive decorations were gorgeous - everything felt warm and inviting despite the December cold. I particularly loved the small, creative decorations scattered throughout the park, like the piano and other musical-themed installations that added such a charming, artistic touch to the whole experience.

The live music was a real highlight! The historic Music Pavilion serves as an open-air concert stage, and hearing live performances in such a beautiful setting really captured the spirit of Advent. You could tell this place was designed with music and culture in mind. However - and this is the big downside - there were simply TOO many people. The crowds made it difficult to move around, enjoy the performances properly, or really soak in the atmosphere. I kept thinking that if it weren't so overcrowded, this would be an absolutely perfect place to listen to live Advent music, maybe even dance a little under the lights, and just enjoy the magical park atmosphere.

The wooden chalets offered traditional Croatian food and drinks, handmade ornaments, and Christmas gifts, but again, the sheer number of people made browsing a bit challenging. If you can visit during off-peak hours (maybe early in the day or on a weekday), I imagine the experience would be much more pleasant and romantic.

**Interesting historical fact:** Advent in Zagreb has an incredible legacy - it was voted "Best Christmas Market in Europe" by European Best Destinations for three consecutive years: 2016, 2017, and 2018! After winning three times, Zagreb received the award the maximum number of times possible and has permanently established itself as one of Europe's premier Christmas destinations. What makes Zagreb's Advent unique is that it's not just one market, but a city-wide celebration with over 25 individual Christmas markets spread across different locations, each with its own unique theme and atmosphere. Advent na Zrinjevcu is considered the most romantic and cozy location, often described as having an "old-fashioned charm" with its musical focus. The park transforms into what locals call a "musical park," with daily concerts ranging from classical performances and jazz to contemporary music, along with special programs like "Špica" featuring retro hits on Saturdays and "Classic at Noon" concerts on Sundays. The tradition of Advent in Zagreb actually spans decades, with the entire Lower Town becoming part of "Lenuci's Green Horseshoe" celebration. The Music Pavilion at Zrinjevac dates back to the 19th century and has been hosting concerts for over a century, making it a truly historic venue for Christmas music. The musical theme is so important to Zrinjevac's Advent that even the decorations often incorporate musical instruments and notes, creating a cohesive artistic vision that sets it apart from other Christmas markets in Europe!

Beautiful location with wonderful atmosphere - just try to visit when it's less crowded for the full magical experience!

reviews.author: Miljenko Šuflaj ml.
reviews.text:
We made an order for KFC, which belongs to a larger food court. Since the KFC inside was too crowded, with no place to sit, we went ahead and sat down in the much emptier area where Foodie and Fisherija operate. We did not take up anyone's spot, and half of the people present also ate KFC/McDonalds.

Then some guy, unsure if he was the restaurant owner, or the doorman, just a bigger, older fella in a vest carrying a plastic bag, came up to us and said that we couldn't eat there, since it's not KFCs area. Alright, we stood up, as did some other people, but since there were no seats available in the KFC, and we saw that other people eating KFC/McDonalds were not turned away, we returned, only to be sent away again. This time, the guy sent away other people too.

So, for him, nothing changes. No new customers arrived, just those that filled up the place were turned away. So now aside from there not being demand, other people can see how empty the place is and start thinking why. We ate the rest of our meal in the car. While I understand that it's KFCs and McD's fault or at least circumstance of not having enough seats for the demand, this is incredibly unhospitable and next time we'll think twice about eating in Avenue Mall. Maybe there's just not enough space for everyone.

Certainly, the guy or the management of those restaurants should know that this didn't make us more likely to eat with them. Or maybe they should visit ex. the Arena Mall where those same business all share their seats and no one is bothering their or their colleagues' customers. At least there you're allowed to commit the sin of eating normie food far from the selling spot.

reviews.author: Denis Sabedinovski

The parking on the ground and underground -1 floor are covered, which is useful during rainy days.
Unfortunately, because of the pandemic it's not permitted to eat in the center, and they have also removed all the benches for taking a break.

reviews.author: Aida Lifestyle Traveler
reviews.text:
Importanne Centar is a well-located shopping spot right in the heart of Zagreb, making it perfect for anyone who wants quick and easy access to a wide range of stores. Its central location is definitely one of its biggest advantages — you can reach it effortlessly whether you’re walking through the city, using public transport, or coming from the train station.

The mall offers a great variety of shops, from everyday essentials to fashion, beauty, gifts, and services. It’s the kind of place where you can find everything you need in one stop. The layout is simple and convenient, and it’s especially useful if you’re in the center and want to do some fast, practical shopping without going too far.

Overall, Importanne Centar is a reliable and convenient shopping destination, ideal for both locals and visitors who appreciate a central location and a solid selection of stores.
